summary:
The Habeas Corpus Act (May 26, 1679 AD) was the English statute that formalized and strengthened the ancient common law writ of habeas corpus — the legal mechanism requiring that a person detained by authorities be brought before a court to determine whether the detention is lawful. 'Habeas corpus' (Latin for 'you shall have the body') had existed in English common law for centuries, but earlier statutes were inconsistently applied; the 1679 Act, passed during the constitutional conflicts of Charles II's reign, established clear procedures and penalties for non-compliance, making the right enforceable in practice. The Act was a direct response to cases where political prisoners had been detained indefinitely without trial, a practice the parliamentary opposition was determined to prevent.

The Habeas Corpus Act became one of the foundational documents of English liberty and influenced constitutional systems worldwide. The right it protected — that no government can imprison a person without bringing them before a court and justifying the imprisonment — became a fundamental principle of liberal governance, incorporated into the [[event:2409|English Bill of Rights]] tradition, the American Constitution (which prohibits suspension of habeas corpus except in cases of rebellion or invasion), and the legal frameworks of most democratic states. The act was suspended during periods of emergency — particularly during Irish rebellions and the Jacobite threats of the 18th century — demonstrating that the right, while foundational, was always contested against state security claims. The Habeas Corpus Act stands alongside Magna Carta (1215), the Petition of Right (1628), and the [[event:2409|English Bill of Rights]] (1689) as one of the pillars of English constitutional law.
